text: I agree about the ink.  The bold aesthetic has a kind of Apple ][ feel.  You'll learn a thing or two from trying to make presentation details modular. text: VR is such a blast!  Wish I was doing more!  So much to explore.  Just reading your paragraphs. ideas come to mind: (1) better experiences use eyes for finding things but do not require them for using things, (2) corollary, using gaze to select a target while holding the object that is going to act on it, (3) allowing for sub-selection of target attributes by dynamically exploding them.  That amounts to the VR equivalent of, say, radial submenus or popup inspectors, expanding drag targets.  What with headset focal distance, manipulation beyond arm's length works out better than close work.  I mean for it it still be direct, just with, I don't know, giant virtual hands...

text: Definitely agree.  I really wanted something close to a radial menu and pitched variants on of it.  Also wanted a dynamic exploding UI (hierarchical zoomable UI in VR) in order to manage complexity.  Ideal focal distance vs. ideal manipulation distance is always a problem in VR and leads to a lot of UI that is optimized for text readability at ideal focal length rather than controller interaction.  This was kind of the reason why the Horizon UI turned into a big floating menu.  The funny thing is for some reason they didn't want laser pointer interaction, so we ended up with a UI that had to be in arms reach but usually couldn't be interacted with at the ideal distance either...

elements.text: Definitely agree.  I really wanted something close to a radial menu and pitched variants on of it.  Also wanted a dynamic exploding UI (hierarchical zoomable UI in VR) in order to manage complexity.  Ideal focal distance vs. ideal manipulation distance is always a problem in VR and leads to a lot of UI that is optimized for text readability at ideal focal length rather than controller interaction.  This was kind of the reason why the Horizon UI turned into a big floating menu.  The funny thing is for some reason they didn't want laser pointer interaction, so we ended up with a UI that had to be in arms reach but usually couldn't be interacted with at the ideal distance either...

text: My ideal UI would have minimized text to the extent that ideal focal length didn't matter, and optimized for pick up and place physical controller interaction.  Although I was pretty married to the idea of a tangible AST editor for coding logic, even that was a compromise, and we didn't really end up with that anyway.  I don't have a lot of faith in the team moving closer to my vision since I've left but I could be surprised when Horizon ships

text: Mathematicians have a notation for case-by-case analysis. See attached image as a random example. Note that it doesn't use colors, borders, or extraneous symbols (other than a bracket and a few "if"s). I think it is extremely legible! Is there a reason not to follow a path closer to this?

text: (I've been gradually learning that borders are over-used. Even though their purpose is just to delineate an inside and an outside, they tend to call attention to themselves and make a noisy image. Spacing & subtle backgrounds tend to work better.)

text:
<@UBN9AFS0N>

> the `=` on the clauses seems to be a star, but after main it's missing
This is intentional. There's not a 1:1 correspondence between the textual symbols and visual symbols. If fizz is written using MultiWayIf (which is slightly closer to the visual representation since Glance only has expressions, not declarations) the = in the clauses become ->

```fizz' x = if
  | x `mod` 15 == 0 -> "FizzBuzz"
  | x `mod` 3  == 0 -> "Fizz"
  | x `mod` 5  == 0 -> "Buzz"
  | otherwise       -> show x```
> in flow diagrams there's a standard symbol for alternative
Is this symbol a diamond? The symbols for the if expression (the half filled circle and the star) are fairly arbitrary, so most any two distinct shapes could work here.

> instead of wrapping everything in boxes, using parenthesis to delimit "boxes"
Great suggestion, I'll try this out.

<@UBKNXPBAB>

> Mathematicians have a notation for case-by-case analysis. [...] Is there a reason not to follow a path closer to this?
I think replacing the conditional symbol (half filled circle) with "if", and the value symbol (orange star) with "then" could work. One difference from the math notation is that oftentimes the value symbol is below the conditional. The bracket is problematic because it would cross over wires going left to right.

text: <@UP3FH4CLU> Instead of doing a fizzbuzz code example, which at its core is a mere 4 lines of code, why not tackle a larger task and see how you would represent it. Although it seems like micro examples are the way to build up towards something, i think it just paints you into a corner over and over again. I have seen many attempts to draw flowchart versions of code, but once you get to 100 lines of code, it becomes a big mess, and people aren't having trouble with a 4 way case statement. So my suggestion is take on a bigger example so that you are immediately confronted with the true problem, which is how to improve comprehensibility using the graphical techniques at your disposal.

text:
<@UEQ6M68H0> Yes, I agree that being able to represent large functions well is important. Although I do also think that there's value in being able to represent smaller bits of code compactly. One strategy for representing large functions is to decompose them into smaller functions. Compact representations of small functions makes this strategy more viable since all of the small functions can be fit close together on the screen.

<@U79HM6726> It's not more compact than the textual Haskell version. As an aside, it's interesting to ponder how to make a visual representation that is more compact than the textual version. Three ideas that come to mind are:
1. No variable names. In this example for instance all of the "x"s could be removed.
2. An understandable visual representation of point-free style code. I have not thought much about this since Glance needs to generate understandable textual code.
3. Use a 3rd dimension. For example space (AR/VR) or time (animations or user interaction).

It's not more compact than the textual Haskell version. As an aside, it's interesting to ponder how to make a visual representation that is more compact than the textual version. Three ideas that come to mind are:
No variable names. In this example for instance all of the "x"s could be removed.
An understandable visual representation of point-free style code. I have not thought much about this since Glance needs to generate understandable textual code.
Use a 3rd dimension. For example space (AR/VR) or time (animations or user interaction).

text:
<@UEQ6M68H0> Yes, I agree that being able to represent large functions well is important. Although I do also think that there's value in being able to represent smaller bits of code compactly. One strategy for representing large functions is to decompose them into smaller functions. Compact representations of small functions makes this strategy more viable since all of the small functions can be fit close together on the screen.

<@U79HM6726> It's not more compact than the textual Haskell version. As an aside, it's interesting to ponder how to make a visual representation that is more compact than the textual version. Three ideas that come to mind are:
1. No variable names. In this example for instance all of the "x"s could be removed.
2. An understandable visual representation of point-free style code. I have not thought much about this since Glance needs to generate understandable textual code.
3. Use a 3rd dimension. For example space (AR/VR) or time (animations or user interaction).

One strategy for representing large functions is to decompose them into smaller functions. Compact representations of small functions makes this strategy more viable since all of the small functions can be fit close together on the screen.

It's not more compact than the textual Haskell version. As an aside, it's interesting to ponder how to make a visual representation that is more compact than the textual version. Three ideas that come to mind are:
No variable names. In this example for instance all of the "x"s could be removed.
An understandable visual representation of point-free style code. I have not thought much about this since Glance needs to generate understandable textual code.
Use a 3rd dimension. For example space (AR/VR) or time (animations or user interaction).

text: You've done a great job exploring the various degrees of compactness. When you watch the  morph sequence where you take the factorial function from its most exploded graphical form and take it all the way to textual, my mind instantly becomes relieved at the textual form resulting. I think for people well trained in reading code, these graphical tools seem like a step in the wrong direction. However for those not yet trained to read code, i would imagine the graphical form holds appeal. I would caution you that one thing you are not exploring is the effect of the code on the state of the systems. In some sense it is the runtime environment that is of interest as it executes. So instead of spending so much time evaluating all the different levels of compactness, perhaps exploring the animation aspects of the program as it runs would be more fruitful.
